summary: Gruber shares his impressions of the Apple TV 4K after a few days of testing, though he admits he can't evaluate its headline feature since he doesn't own a 4K TV. He praises the simplified setup process and improved performance but criticizes Apple's unchanged remote control design. The bulk of his analysis focuses on technical limitations highlighted by Nilay Patel's Verge review: the lack of Dolby Atmos support, missing 4K YouTube due to VP9 codec incompatibility, and problematic HDR upscaling of non-iTunes content. Gruber argues that Apple TV's small market share won't be enough to force Google into supporting H.265 for YouTube, and that Apple will likely have to cave on VP9 support. He concludes that the upgrade decision is simple: get it if you have a 4K TV, skip it otherwise.
key_points: Apple's refusal to redesign the widely disliked remote control is baffling given the company's design reputation
The Apple TV 4K is a faster computer than even the maxed-out Mac Mini, highlighting how neglected the Mac Mini lineup has become
YouTube's VP9 codec standoff puts Apple at a disadvantage because Apple TV lacks the market share to force Google's hand
Apple TV's premium pricing creates premium expectations that its technical limitations (no Atmos, no 4K YouTube, inconsistent HDR upscaling) fail to meet
The HDR upscaling of non-iTunes 1080p content is problematic, with blown-out contrast and excessive sharpening in third-party apps
money_quotes: It is baffling to me that Apple didn't redesign the remote control to make it obvious at a touch which way it's oriented.
Nobody loves this remote. Most people I know outright dislike it. And Apple left it almost unchanged.
Unlike the iPhone and iPad, Apple TV doesn't have enough market share to force Google's hand.
YouTube's enormous popularity is more likely to force Apple into supporting VP9 than the Apple TV's middling popularity is to force YouTube into supporting H.265.
The whole point of Apple TV is that — like with all Apple products — its entire reason for existence is to provide a premium experience for discerning users.
Apple may well have good technical or legal reasons for not supporting VP9. Apple TV users don't care. They just want YouTube videos to look great on their TVs.
You could arrange four Apple TV units in a 2 × 2 square and they'd take up the same volume as one Mac Mini.
That's some serious James Bond villain's lair shit.
key_insight: Apple TV 4K's premium price sets premium expectations, but technical compromises around codec support, HDR processing, and an unchanged disliked remote undermine the very premium experience that justifies Apple's existence in this market.
